text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Robert Delacroix | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Oncologist, on file]
Visit Type: Established Patient Follow-Up — Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer, Disease Reassessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for reassessment of mCRPC on enzalutamide. Patient presents with new-onset thoracic back pain and biochemical/radiographic progression documented on most recent restaging studies.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix is a 71-year-old retired civil engineer with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M1b at initial diagnosis) who has been followed in this clinic over the past several years. He was originally diagnosed with mHSPC with polyostotic bone metastases (L-spine, pelvis, right femur) and pelvic nodal involvement, and was initiated on ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone acetate (Zytiga)/prednisone, with excellent initial PSA response (87.4 → nadir 0.8 ng/mL). Approximately 12 months ago he developed biochemical CRPC on abiraterone and was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, with ADT continued. He achieved a 93% PSA decline to a nadir of 0.6 ng/mL at approximately month 5. PSA remained low at 0.9 ng/mL at his 6-month assessment and disease was considered radiographically stable.

He now returns approximately 9 months into enzalutamide therapy. Serial PSA values over the past 3 months have shown unmistakable upward trend: 1.4 → 3.8 → 9.2 ng/mL, meeting biochemical progression criteria while confirmed castrate (testosterone 8 ng/dL). A technetium-99m bone scan performed approximately 2 weeks ago showed new areas of radiotracer uptake at T10 and T12 vertebral bodies and the right posterior 4th rib, confirming radiographic progression. Previously known osseous lesions in the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur show a mixed picture.

Of note, over the past 3-4 weeks the patient has developed new thoracic back pain rated 4-5/10, located mid-thoracic and radiating around the right chest wall, consistent with the newly identified T10 and T12 lesions. Pain is worse with movement and lying flat; partially relieved with acetaminophen/ibuprofen alternating. He denies any lower extremity weakness, numbness, tingling, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or difficulty with ambulation. No fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss beyond what has been previously noted.

Given that he carries a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT, confirmed on prior NGS workup with somatic LOH in tumor), and he has now progressed on two second-generation androgen receptor signaling inhibitors (abiraterone then enzalutamide), he meets eligibility criteria for olaparib (Lynparza) per the PROfound trial indication. This was discussed in detail with Mr. Delacroix and his wife at today's visit.

Immediately prior to today's visit, an urgent spine MRI (thoracic and lumbar, with and without contrast) was ordered to assess for epidural extension and cord compromise given the new thoracic lesions and back pain, and is pending result at time of this dictation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive for: thoracic back pain (grade 2, 4-5/10), right chest wall discomfort with deep breathing, fatigue (grade 1-2, worsened somewhat over the past month), decreased exercise tolerance, persistent hot flashes (grade 2-3, chronic on ADT), mild dizziness (grade 1, baseline on enzalutamide, stable), dry mouth (grade 1).
Negative for: lower extremity weakness or paresthesias, bowel or bladder incontinence, urinary retention, dysphagia, visual changes, hemoptysis, hematemesis, melena, new lymphadenopathy, shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, headache, confusion. No fall since last visit. No fractures.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: Blood pressure 136/82 mmHg (on amlodipine 5 mg), heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.7°C, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 91 kg.
General: Alert, oriented x3, well-groomed. Appropriately anxious about today's findings but engaged and asking good questions.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. Oropharynx clear.
Neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. 1+ bilateral lower extremity edema, stable from prior visits.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No percussion dullness.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. No ascites.
Musculoskeletal/Neurological: Tenderness to palpation over T10-T12 spinous processes and paraspinous musculature. Reproducible right chest wall discomfort on rib palpation at level of 4th rib laterally. No step-off deformity. Gait stable, narrow-based. Lower extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally in hip flexors, knee extensors, ankle dorsiflexion/plantar flexion. DTRs 2+ and symmetric. Babinski negative bilaterally. No saddle anesthesia on targeted exam.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy.

---

LABORATORY DATA (CURRENT VISIT)
PSA: 9.2 ng/mL (up from nadir 0.6 ng/mL; 3-month trend: 1.4 → 3.8 → 9.2)
Total testosterone: 8 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
CBC: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild decline from prior 11.8 g/dL), Platelets 204 x10^9/L
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139, Potassium 4.1, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~64), ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 184 U/L (mildly elevated, chronic osseous involvement), Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 3.9 g/dL
LDH: 218 U/L (mildly elevated)
Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Bone scan (Tc-99m, performed approximately 2 weeks ago): New areas of increased radiotracer uptake at T10 and T12 vertebral bodies and right posterior 4th rib — new since the prior bone scan performed at 9 months prior. Previously known lesions in lumbar spine (L2-L4), pelvis, and right femur show a somewhat mixed picture with some lesions appearing more prominent. Overall impression: new bone metastases consistent with progression.

Spine MRI (thoracic/lumbar, with and without contrast): Results pending at time of this dictation; stat read requested.

---

ASSESSMENT
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9, germline BRCA2 mutant — radiographic and biochemical progression on enzalutamide.
   - PSA has risen from nadir 0.6 to 9.2 ng/mL over 3 months, with confirmed castrate testosterone. New osseous metastases at T10, T12, and right 4th rib on bone scan. Progression is clear after approximately 9 months on Xtandi, following prior abiraterone/prednisone progression.
   - Patient has received two lines of second-generation ARSI (abiraterone, then enzalutamide). AR-V7 status not yet formally tested on current sample (was not tested at last progression), but clinical behavior consistent with ARSI resistance. NGS from diagnostic biopsy confirmed germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with somatic LOH.
   - Given mCRPC with BRCA2 biallelic loss, patient is eligible for olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID per PROfound data showing radiographic PFS benefit in HRR-mutant CRPC post-ARSI.

2. New thoracic back pain, T10/T12 vertebral metastases with possible epidural involvement — MRI pending.
   - Neurological exam today is reassuring (full lower extremity strength, intact sensation, normal reflexes, ambulating independently). Will not initiate high-dose steroids empirically at this time pending MRI result.
   - If MRI demonstrates cord compromise or significant epidural disease, will urgently refer to radiation oncology and/or neurosurgery.
   - Palliative radiation oncology referral being placed today regardless, given T10/T12 involvement and pain.

3. Anemia, mild (Hgb 11.2 g/dL) — trending slightly down, likely multifactorial (marrow infiltration, chronic disease, ADT). Will monitor closely when olaparib initiated given known myelosuppressive potential.

4. Hypertension — stable on amlodipine 5 mg daily. BP acceptable today.

---

PLAN
1. DISCONTINUE enzalutamide 160 mg daily effective today. Discussed rationale with patient.

2. INITIATE olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg orally twice daily with food, once MRI results are reviewed and spine stability confirmed. Patient counseled extensively regarding:
   - Common side effects: fatigue, nausea/vomiting, anemia and cytopenias (CBC monitoring schedule reviewed — every 4 weeks x 3 months, then every 2-3 months)
   - Rare but serious risks: MDS/AML (low, but discussed), pneumonitis (rare), embryo-fetal toxicity (not applicable)
   - Drug interactions reviewed; no significant interactions with current medications
   - No concurrent use of strong CYP3A inhibitors/inducers
   - Prior authorization submitted

3. ADT with leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months — CONTINUE. Next injection due in approximately 6 weeks.

4. Zoledronic acid (Reclast) 4 mg IV q3 months — CONTINUE for bone-directed therapy. Dental clearance up to date. Renal function adequate.

5. Spine MRI results will be reviewed today/tomorrow. If T10 or T12 shows significant epidural disease or cord compression:
   - Urgent radiation oncology consultation for emergent EBRT
   - Neurosurgery co-management if indicated
   - Dexamethasone to be initiated if cord compromise confirmed
   Regardless of MRI result, palliative EBRT referral placed to radiation oncology for pain control at T10/T12.

6. Pain management: Acetaminophen 1000 mg q6h scheduled (ok with current liver function) plus ibuprofen 400 mg PRN with food (short-term, monitor renal function). If pain escalates or MRI shows significant structural compromise, low-dose oxycodone PRN to be considered — discussed briefly with patient, who is amenable.

7. Laboratory follow-up: PSA, CBC with differential, CMP, LDH in 4 weeks after olaparib initiation. Testosterone quarterly.

8. Goals of care: Had a meaningful conversation with Mr. Delacroix and his wife today. Reiterated incurable but treatable nature of disease. Emphasized that next-line therapy with olaparib offers real potential for benefit given his BRCA2 status. Patient understands disease trajectory; expresses preference for maintaining quality of life and time at home. Palliative care co-management will be maintained.

9. Return to clinic in 4-6 weeks after starting olaparib, or sooner if pain worsens, new neurological symptoms, or significant side effects.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 55 minutes, greater than 50% in discussion of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment planning.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Oncologist, on file]
Hematology/Oncology
